title: Employee Lookup via Pointer
prompt_md:
## Goal
Search a list of employees for one with a given ID. Track the match with a pointer to a struct (`nullptr` when there is no match), and print the result through a function that accesses members with the arrow operator.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 10), the number of employees
- Line 2: an integer `queryId`, the ID to search for
- Next `n` lines: each contains a name (single word) and an integer ID, space-separated

## Output
- If an employee with `queryId` exists:
  ```
  Found: <name> (ID: <id>)
  ```
- Otherwise:
  ```
  Not found
  ```

## Constraints
- Define a `struct Employee` with `std::string name` and `int id`.
- Keep track of the matching employee using an `Employee*` (start it at `nullptr`).
- Print the found employee through a function that receives a pointer and uses the arrow operator (`->`); do not use `(*ptr).member`.
- All IDs in the input are distinct. Do not use arrays.

test_cases.label: Hidden -- match is the last employee

title: Modify Structs Through Pointers and Read Through References
prompt_md:
## Goal
Double a rectangle's dimensions through a pointer (using the arrow operator), then print it through a const reference (using the dot operator).

## Input
- Line 1: two integers `width` and `height` (1 <= each <= 1000)

## Output
Two lines:
```
Before: <width> x <height> (area: <area>)
After: <width> x <height> (area: <area>)
```
The "After" line shows the rectangle once both dimensions have been doubled.

## Constraints
- Define a `struct Rect` with `int width` and `int height`.
- Write a function that takes a `Rect*` and doubles both dimensions via the arrow operator (`->`).
- Write a function that takes a `const Rect&` and prints `<width> x <height> (area: <area>)` (ending the line) via the dot operator. The `Before: `/`After: ` prefixes are printed by the caller, not inside the printing function.
